trajectory_summary: The call opened neutral and collapsed in under two minutes. Troy's ad-promise objection at 01:33 was the pivot point — Bucky's procedural restatement of the credit threshold instead of an honest acknowledgment of the ad-framing gap allowed Troy's sales-veteran skepticism to harden into an adversarial frame that never softened. Every subsequent move (self-defense, program explanation, panel description) was deployed against an unresolved objection and read as confirmation of Troy's 'oldest trick in the book' verdict. The call ended with the original objection still on the table.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ended with no appointment booked and an explicit customer exit ('scratch me off that list'). Outcome should be tagged: no_appointment / lost_lead. No dispute with that read — the call did not produce a bookable outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Ad-promise objection resolution — acknowledge before explaining
primary_coaching_focus.quote: the ad that I saw was saying there was no charge. So what why would I need a credit score?
primary_coaching_focus.why: This objection surfaced at 01:33 and was never resolved — Troy's final line at 05:14 is still the same objection. Bucky's pattern across three touches was to explain the program rather than acknowledge the contradiction Troy experienced. The manager should run Bucky through a single-sentence acknowledgment drill: 'You're right — the ad language is misleading. No upfront cost is not the same as free. Here's the actual difference.' Until Bucky can lead with acknowledgment before explanation, every credit-question objection on a 'free solar' lead will follow this same trajectory.

key_moments.quote: it it shouldn't be nothing to qualify for.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky's double negative ('shouldn't be nothing') is the first and best opportunity to explain the financing logic clearly — and he fumbles it before Troy's sales-veteran frame has fully hardened. At this moment Troy is still asking a question ('why would I need a credit score?'), not yet issuing a verdict. A clean explanation of how solar financing works (no upfront cost, monthly payment replaces the bill, credit qualifies the financing — not the panels) could have resolved the contradiction. Instead, Bucky's garbled restatement confirmed Troy's suspicion that something was being obscured.

key_moments.quote: I've been in sales my whole life. Okay. I understand how that works. So, you know, that's that's that's the oldest trick in the book.
key_moments.stewart_read: Troy's sales-veteran credential is an adversarial frame, not a vulnerability disclosure. The correct read here is an inversion opportunity: Troy's sales experience is the lever, not the obstacle. A rep who says 'Then you know better than anyone that the only way to know if the numbers work is to see the actual numbers — not the ad copy' is using Troy's frame against itself. Bucky instead self-defended ('I'm not a salesman'), which is exactly what someone running the 'oldest trick' would say. The inversion was available; Bucky didn't see it.

key_moments.quote: It's probably AI. It's probably fake anyways.
key_moments.stewart_read: Troy's 'Tesla's solar panels, Elon Musk ad, probably AI, probably fake' is a compound legitimacy challenge — he's questioning the ad source, the Tesla connection, and the entire call's authenticity simultaneously. This is the scam_anxiety_signal the schema flags. The correct move is to anchor the call as customer-initiated and clarify the Tesla relationship cleanly: 'Tesla forwarded your inquiry to us — we're their certified installer in your state. You filled out a form; that's why I'm calling.' Bucky does not address this at all and moves directly into the program pitch, which reads as confirmation of Troy's fraud thesis.

key_moments.quote: We have the we're partnered with Tesla, and we use the all black panels, and they're very pretty. They're very sturdy. They're, like, literally bulletproof, and they they work great.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky deploys panel aesthetics and durability ('very pretty,' 'literally bulletproof') to a customer who has already said he's not interested and is actively questioning the call's legitimacy. This is the anti-pattern the schema names explicitly: explaining features to a skeptical customer creates more objections, not fewer. Troy's response ('Yeah. But no. I'm not interested in running a credit report') confirms the features pitch did nothing. The panel description also misrepresents Ion's product line — Ion sells its own panels, not Tesla panels (context.ion_offerings_beyond_setter_call). 'Partnered with Tesla' is accurate framing; 'we use the all black panels' in this context implies Tesla panels.
